The implementation uses a 1‑month lookback period for colonoscopy, which is inconsistent with clinical guidelines that recommend colonoscopy screening every 10 years (or 5 years for high‑risk patients). This would trigger a screening recommendation for any patient aged ≥50 who has not had a colonoscopy in the past month, leading to over‑screening and not reflecting the intended 10‑year interval.

; The implementation uses an age threshold of **≥25 years**, whereas most hypertension screening guidelines recommend screening adults **≥18 years**.; The lookback period for “Encounter to Screen for Blood Pressure” is **6 months**; typical guidelines suggest a screening interval of **12 months** (or at least once per year).; The Value Set ID **2.16.840.1.113883.3.600.1920** is not verified against the standard encounter type for blood‑pressure screening and may not match the intended CPG value set.

; The HbA1c threshold of **≥ 9 g%** is not clinically reasonable for a type‑2 diabetes diagnosis; standard guidelines recommend a threshold of **≥ 6.5 %** (or 48 mmol/mol).; The entry labeled “Time Intervals (Lookback Periods)” incorrectly lists **9 g%**; this is a laboratory value, not a time interval.

; The implementation does not enforce the 20‑pack‑year smoking history requirement that is part of the USPSTF guideline.; The “Has Smoking History” logic filters for a condition with `clinicalStatus.coding[0].code = active`, which would exclude former smokers who still meet the 20‑pack‑year criterion.

Age thresholds – the ELM specifies Minimum Age as 60–75 years, whereas the guideline recommends screening for average‑risk adults aged 50–75 years; Lookback period – the ELM uses a 2‑year lookback for “Has Recent Colorectal Screening,” but colorectal cancer screening intervals vary by modality (e.g., colonoscopy every 10 years, FIT annually), so a 2‑year window is not clinically appropriate.

; **Age threshold mismatch** – The ELM requires patients to be ≥30 years old, whereas most HIV screening guidelines recommend testing adults aged 15–65 (or all adults).; **Lookback period mismatch** – The ELM uses a 1‑year lookback to determine a recent test, but guidelines typically recommend screening at least once every 3 years for adults, not annually.
The BMI threshold for obesity is set at 35 kg/m², whereas clinical guidelines define obesity as BMI ≥ 30 kg/m². This makes the “Is Obese” logic too restrictive for prediabetes screening.
The ELM implementation sets the lower age threshold to 30 years, whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends cervical cancer screening for women aged 21–65 years. The lower bound should be 21, not 30.

The age threshold in the ELM implementation (≥ 75 years) does not align with the USPSTF guideline, which recommends exercise interventions for community‑dwelling adults **≥ 65 years** who are at increased risk for falls.

The ELM implementation requires patients to be at least 25 years old to be considered for screening, whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends screening adults 18 years and older. This age threshold mismatch makes the logic clinically inconsistent with the guideline.

The ELM implementation uses an age lower bound of 55 years for screening eligibility, whereas the USPSTF guideline recommends one‑time AAA screening for men aged **65–75** years who have ever smoked. This lower age threshold is clinically incorrect.

The logic for determining who needs screening is incomplete.; **Needs Tobacco Use Screening** is defined only as `[In Demographic]`, which means every adult 18+ is considered in need of screening, regardless of whether they have had a recent screening.; Consequently, **InPopulation** (and therefore the recommendation, rationale, and indicator) will apply to all adults 18+, which is not clinically appropriate.; The logic should exclude patients who have had a recent tobacco use screening (e.g., `In Demographic AND NOT Has Recent Tobacco Screening`).

; The implementation does not enforce the upper age limit of 80 years required by USPSTF for lung cancer screening.; The smoking history criterion is insufficient; USPSTF requires a 20 pack‑year smoking history, but the logic only checks for any active tobacco user condition.; The smoking history logic does not verify pack‑year exposure or duration of smoking.

; The lookback period for a colonoscopy is set to **1 month**, which is not clinically appropriate for screening intervals (colonoscopies are typically performed every 10 years for average‑risk adults).; The logic requires a colonoscopy performed within the last month to trigger a recommendation for screening, which is contradictory—if a colonoscopy has just been done, no additional screening is needed.; Consequently, the recommendation logic is reversed: it recommends a colonoscopy when one has already been performed recently.; (Optional) The age threshold of 50 years may be outdated; current USPSTF guidelines recommend initiating screening at age 45 for average‑risk adults.

The logic for screening is inconsistent with the recommendation.; **Needs Falls Prevention Screening** is defined as `[In Demographic] AND [Has Recent Falls Assessment]`, meaning the patient must already have a recent falls assessment to be considered for further screening.; The **Recommendation** then instructs to *perform a falls risk assessment* and refer to exercise intervention if at increased risk, which contradicts the screening condition (the patient already has a recent assessment).; Clinically, the screening should target patients who are 65+ **and have NOT had a recent falls assessment** (i.e., missing or outdated assessment). The current logic incorrectly requires a recent assessment, leading to a mismatch between the screening trigger and the recommended action.

The implementation uses an age threshold of ≥12 years for all patients, which would trigger depression screening for adults older than 65 years. USPSTF recommends screening for adults only up to 65 years of age (18–65 years). Additionally, the logic does not differentiate between adult and adolescent screening beyond the tool recommendation, potentially leading to inappropriate screening for older adults.
